_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Hoke County, NC. Physical geography, sensory snapshots, and landscape change narratives for North Carolina's youngest county — carved from Robeson in 1911, defined by Fort Bragg and the Lumber River Sandhills.
display_name: Hoke County, North Carolina — Landscape Profile

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Sandhills subregion of the Atlantic Coastal Plain. Hoke County is deep sandhills country — flat to gently rolling terrain on ancient marine sand deposits, drained by the headwaters of the Lumber River and Rockfish Creek. Fort Bragg dominates the northern portion.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 200–500 ft. Gentle sandhills terrain with no significant elevation changes. The landscape is monotonously flat compared to the Piedmont but subtly rolling compared to the lower coastal plain.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Sandy pine flats and gently rolling sandhills. Fort Bragg's military reservation covers much of the northern half of the county. The southern half is agricultural — tobacco, cotton, and corn on the better soils, pine forest on the deep sand.

soil_types.description: Deep, excessively drained fine sand. The dominant soil of the Sandhills uplands. White to pale yellow.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Poor for crops — too sandy, too draining. Supported longleaf pine and little else. Families who tried to farm Candor sand learned that the soil would not hold fertilizer or moisture long enough for a crop to use either.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, NC (1920; rev. 1985).

soil_types.description: Yellowish-brown sandy loam in stream bottoms and lower slopes. Better water retention than the upland sands.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: The only farmable soil in most of Hoke County. Tobacco and corn grew in the creek bottoms where Norfolk sandy loam accumulated. Farm settlement followed the streams.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, NC (1920; rev. 1985).

major_waterways.name: Rockfish Creek
major_waterways.role: Northern drainage toward Fayetteville and the Cape Fear
major_waterways.description: Rockfish Creek flows northeast through Hoke County toward the Cape Fear River at Fayetteville. Its valley provided the natural corridor connecting the Sandhills to the Upper Cape Fear market. Fort Bragg's reservation encompasses much of the upper Rockfish watershed.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: Rockfish Creek was {ancestor_name}'s road to Fayetteville — the drainage that connected Hoke County's deep sandhills to the market at the Cape Fear.

major_waterways.name: Lumber River (headwaters)
major_waterways.description: The headwaters of the Lumber River rise in the southern Sandhills of Hoke County. The Lumber is a blackwater stream — dark with tannins from cypress and swamp vegetation. It flows south through Robeson County toward the Pee Dee and eventually the Atlantic.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Lumber River's headwaters in southern Hoke County ran dark as tea — a blackwater stream that drained the poorest sand country in the cluster and connected the Sandhills to the Lumbee homeland in Robeson County.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Longleaf pine savanna with wiregrass understory
vegetation_eras.description: Virtually unbroken longleaf pine on deep sand, maintained by natural fire. The Sandhills of what would become Hoke County were among the most pristine longleaf ecosystems in the Southeast — too poor and remote for early settlement.
vegetation_eras.source: Earley, Lawrence S. Looking for Longleaf: The Fall and Rise of an American Forest. UNC Press, 2004.

vegetation_eras.description: Naval stores operations worked the longleaf, but settlement was sparse. The area that became Hoke County was the remote interior of Cumberland and Robeson counties — far from markets, deep in sand, and thinly populated.

Hoke County was created in 1911 and immediately defined by its poverty and remoteness. Fort Bragg's establishment in 1918 consumed the best-connected portion of the county, leaving the remainder as marginal agricultural land and pine forest. The military reservation paradoxically preserved longleaf pine habitat that would otherwise have been logged.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Raeford in summer was a small courthouse town baking on white sand. The county had been carved from Robeson barely a decade earlier, and the town was still more ambition than substance — a courthouse, a few stores along Main Street, a cotton gin, and frame houses scattered among the pines. The heat pressed down through the longleaf canopy and the wiregrass crackled underfoot. Beyond the town limits in every direction, the sandhills stretched in their monotonous flatness — pine, sand, wiregrass, the occasional tobacco field in a creek bottom. Fort Bragg's artillery practice rumbled from the north like distant thunder that never produced rain.
sensory_snapshots.source: Hoke County Heritage Book Committee. Heritage of Hoke County. Raeford, NC: 1988.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Before it was Hoke County, this was the deep interior of Robeson and Cumberland — turpentine country where the camps were scattered through the longleaf and the nearest town was a day's ride. In winter the pines stood bare of undergrowth after the fall fires had burned through, and the white sand showed between the wiregrass clumps like bones. Smoke from tar kilns drifted between the trunks. The workers — enslaved before the war, held by debt after it — moved through the trees on a circuit, chipping the boxed pines and collecting crude resin in buckets that they hauled to the still. The only sounds were ax strokes, the wind in the needles, and the occasional cry of a red-cockaded woodpecker nesting in an old-growth pine.
sensory_snapshots.source: Outland, Tapping the Pines (2004); Earley, Looking for Longleaf (2004).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: The convoys running through Raeford in the fall of 1943 shook the windows of every building on Main Street. Fort Bragg had swallowed the northern half of the county, and the roads between Raeford and Fayetteville were choked with Army trucks, jeeps, and personnel carriers. The old turpentine country had become an artillery impact area. Local men were overseas or in training. Women worked at the base and in the fields. The cotton gin still ran, but the county's economic center of gravity had shifted permanently from the land to the installation.
sensory_snapshots.source: Lemmon, North Carolina's Role in World War II (1964).

landscape_changes.description: Hoke County was created from Robeson County in 1911, one of the last counties formed in North Carolina. The new county was poor, sandy, and remote. Raeford was designated the county seat. Genealogical records before 1911 are in Robeson County.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: An ancestor in what became Hoke County before 1911 will appear in Robeson County records. The county's late creation reflects its marginal economic status — there was not enough population or tax base to justify a separate county until the early twentieth century.
landscape_changes.source: Powell, William S. The North Carolina Gazetteer. 2nd ed. UNC Press, 2010.

landscape_changes.change: Fort Bragg consumes northern Hoke County
landscape_changes.description: The establishment of Fort Bragg in 1918 and its massive WWII expansion took thousands of acres from northern Hoke County. Families who had farmed the creek bottoms and worked the pine forests were bought out by the government. The military reservation became the county's dominant landscape feature and, paradoxically, its largest employer.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: Fort Bragg split Hoke County families into before and after. Ancestors whose farms were acquired by the military in 1918 or 1940-42 experienced a displacement as total as Sherman's march — the land simply ceased to be theirs.
landscape_changes.source: Lemmon, North Carolina's Role in World War II (1964).

narrative_hooks: Hoke County did not exist until 1911 — if {ancestor_name} lived in this part of the Sandhills before then, look in Robeson County's records, because this ground was Robeson's poorest corner.

Fort Bragg took the northern half of Hoke County in {year}, and {ancestor_name}'s farm went with it — bought by the government for artillery ranges on land too sandy to grow much besides longleaf pine and too remote for anyone else to want.

The sandhills of Hoke County were turpentine country before they were anything else — and when {ancestor_name} arrived in {year}, the boxed pines and tar kilns were the only industry the deep sand would support.

Raeford was barely a town when Hoke County was carved from Robeson in 1911 — a courthouse, a few stores, and an optimism that the new county would amount to more than the last patch of longleaf pine nobody else had claimed.

The Lumber River rose in the southern sandhills of Hoke County and ran dark through swamp and pine flat toward Robeson — and {ancestor_name} may have followed that same dark water south when the Army took the land to the north.
source_references: Earley, Lawrence S. Looking for Longleaf: The Fall and Rise of an American Forest. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2004.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004.
Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.
Hoke County Heritage Book Committee. Heritage of Hoke County. Raeford, NC: 1988.
Powell, William S. The North Carolina Gazetteer. 2nd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, North Carolina. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1920; revised 1985.
